body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

body.title: Delegated Credentials to Host Encrypted DNS Forwarders on CPEs
Delegated Credentials to Host Encrypted DNS Forwarders on CPEs

Last Call stage but we'll get that fixed so uh just add two weeks on to August 3rd and that's when we'll close both those uh calls we have a very short agenda today so we have one draft to be presented by Dan Dan wing on the delegated credentials to host encrypted DNS folders on CPS we need to work on the shortening of these titles they're getting too long all right Dan come on up does that work s do I have to unshare and then okay okay sure [Music] all right I'm Dan wing and this is delegated credentials to host encrypted DNS forwarders on CPE next slide um so what we're trying to do is get local CPE to to support encrypted DNS so that we can get all the usual features out of a localized DNS resolution on the local network but we run into a couple of problems trying to do this and those are at the bottom uh one is the ca science certificates difficult to get uh we'll talk about that a little bit more detail on another slide and um the uh yeah let's let's move on to another slide that sounds good um so uh DDR and DNR are uh nice approaches a problem with DDR though is the the isps propensity to change IP addresses and rotate IP addresses means once we get one that is signed it may change we don't know when that's going to occur and when it does occur when we do get the the public IP address changed we need to go off and fetch a new certificate and during that time we can't offer encrypted DNS to the local network which kind of blows um we also of course can't uh get a signature for a 192 private address um the same problem with V6 um DNR uh works with fqdn which is cool we can go get names and and the ISP and managed uh CPE operator um could have serial numbers or account numbers or something and generate unique fqdns for each one of their CPE and that works all right but in deployment it was found with uh let's encrypt that they don't like serving out that many uh fqdns signing that many ftdns in in a one Fell Swoop and then renewing them comes up with a similar sort of problem so this is where um we we poked around at how else we might solve this other than talking to a CA for every single CPE next slide um yeah and then the last problem that I didn't talk about yet which is that the CPE are often unavailable and unplugged um kind of a small problem because once they're plugged in they can go in and get you know go and and uh uh talk to DNS talk to a CA or talk to uh the CPE management system and get some things but it is it is a problem and delays providing that encrypted DNS next slide please so the two mechanisms we investigated for this and and we chose subserts because of the the pros and cons that it has are subserts and name constraints so name constraints has been around for a long while uh requires CA support but there's very few Cas that support it and not a lot of implementations that we were able to find subserts on the left doesn't require CA support which is nice there's a small change to the TLs client and server that are needed they have to send a a new TLS extension uh that but what's nice is that's out of the out of the cryptography necessary for TLS so it's just in you know it's in the handshake and not in a scary part of modifying TLS um and there is some client support So Firefox supports this today um and I saw that uh the the Facebook home page supports it as well so kind of cool uh there is existing implementation experience with it not in exactly where we need it of course but there's some implementation experience better than nothing next slide please um so this is this is the high high level view of how this would work um when the CPE is plugged in and comes up uh it talks to the managed CPE service so oftentimes the ISP that the CPE is associated with but it doesn't need to be the ISP it can be a separate service that uh you know Asus Netgear or whatever operate as a side effect of you purchasing an Asus or Netgear security enabled Box Thing uh and it talks to the managed CPE service gets the delegated credential brought down and this is what to the CPE and this is what avoids having to talk to the ca and then that is what's presented locally to the encrypted DNS client and that means the encrypted DNS client uh the blue box is everything in the in the home or in the managed you know in the the local network uh it means the encrypted DNS client needs to support the delegated credential for this to work this authentication to work so it knows it's talking to that CPE it validates that and then moves forward that's it I am ready for questions okay Tim you're you're out the mic so can we see notes but you're at the mic what's that I was making a joke as I don't know who's taking notes while you're at the mic oh wait is Ben first no you are go ahead oh I am first okay um because so um I work for a US Cable company we will never give they never give out fqdns to cpes so it's like they'll do like they'll do it for pointers but not for that's like let's not talk about that but can you run your own private CA because we have our own like CPE devices that we maybe build with other you know vendors and stuff and I can see us running something as sort of our own sort of custom CA um to give these out but problematic yes no you know I guess doing sort of internal Cas and sort of uh so it is a problem yeah and also we're doing a lot of dual stack net stuff now too so I I I need to go read how if there's that that's going to affect what you guys are doing but I don't think it will right so we don't care what's going on with the ca which would be to the right and off of this diagram it's it's out of scope because it it doesn't need we don't need any features from the ca so if it's a private CA that that's cool okay Ben Schwartz meta um so I I think if anybody has implemented DNR in a client today then this draft is a non-starter uh if nobody has implemented DNR in a client yet then maybe this draft has a chance because if anybody has implemented DNR in a client today and hasn't also implemented support for delegated credentials then they're going to you you have created a situation where that client walks onto your network uh connects gets a DHCP DNR or however or ra DNR or whatever and then uh attempts to connect gets a TLS handshake failure presumes an attack and drops the connection which means it has no DNS service on this network agreed so uh I I think that we need to sort of answer that question and decide if this is basically we have two options if you want to be able to do this one of them is to update DNR really fast uh I guess it hasn't it doesn't have an RC number yet we ain't get back from the RFC editor and try to modify it uh and the other is to Define some kind of extension so that you can that has to be or basically Define a new DHCP code point and a new ra code point to say like okay this is DNR but it does require support for delegated credentials so if you don't recognize that you have you can you'll just ignore it because otherwise you'd have the staircase yeah I avoided talking about it in the presentation but in the draft is a small section which I I don't like uh on how to deal with exactly that problem and it and it kind of hand waves I would say at uh do keyless SSL and and basically some other sort of fallback mechanisms all which kind of bring in all the problems I talked about on slide three yeah in particular I want to the reason that I think this is especially important to focus on is that I don't want us to sleepwalk into the situation where this is deployed and there's you know therefore situations where this incompatibility is happening because in that case what will inevitably I think end up with is that the clients will have to take sort of a soft fail approach where they they've used DNR they've done the TLs handshake they've gotten the failure the the like certificate validation failure and or whatever and then they can't afford to actually break and so they'll have to come up with a soft failure and if the client if we're encouraging clients to have these very soft failure modes then we're reducing the security that this whole system offered in the first groups and that's good point there's already enough pressure for soft failures to occur I mean yes I recognize there's already already a lot of pressure to make those failures soft but let's not increase that pressure agreed okay Tommy Paul he's next thank you Tommy Polly Apple um I agree with the point Ben was making I don't think you wouldn't we would we should necessarily go and like pull DNR back or that it would really require new DHCP and rra code points my initial impression is could we not instead hear Define a parameter for the svcb parameters and make that mandatory if we think it's going to break such that like all clients would just fail to parse it if they don't understand that there's like this thing uses different credentials you're you're right the man that the SBC be mandatory parameter saves us again sign on that thing um so that was one comment and then just around the document itself it's not just reading through it scanning through it um it's not clear to me which part of this as it's defined as like a standards track thing like is the normative spec here like it talks about you know there's DDR and DNR I talked about delegated credentials um but as far as putting them together it actually I couldn't find like so to clarify like this only works for DNR yes this will only work for DNR okay um so I think it'd be good to spell that out I don't know a ton I haven't looked into the delegated credentials much in detail but right I'm assuming there's nothing we could do to have it kind of dynamically include coverage of the IP that it has so that DDR would work uh we probably I I don't know I have to look at that yeah because I would be curious I mean DDR does leave it somewhat open to say that you have to verify the AP address one way to do that is to verify the IP in this part of the cert but you could have other mechanisms to do that so it would be interesting to see if we could have something that we're able to sign in here to say that yes like at this moment the IP address you're getting is also somehow fine um okay and Eric your next before you do that I will ask you to please refrain from high fiving this is a very serious working group sir when I saw that high five you guys did back there yeah um said um since Tommy raised one of already addressed one of the things I was going to raise the um like I think there's a need for this I will also say if we can find a way to get the to deal with the IP address or the case here for the other mode that would be nice just because in starting kind of in the the starting to play with this on that side that's also turning out to be as painful or more painful than we already expected to be and that was already painful um and it can be a problem but the I may for the CPE case I'm wondering if this is one where it's worth also thinking at a bigger picture of whether there's a better like a more General solution for this because I think this is one that I keep seeing over and over again is whenever people want to run kind of um trust trust your TLS within their within kind of their home network environment we don't have a good solution there and um having a kind of we can one-off adhere to get something out but there's still going to be a lot of other cases where it would be nice to not have to keep what kind of coming up solution like think about that every time I connect to my stupid printer and my stupid Nas and my stupid 192 168 1 1 router and I get a certificate Challenge and yeah that would be awesome and it's clearly it's kind of clearly outside of the TR it's like um as far outside the charter of aad as you can get but it would be also nice if like um if if there's interest if we could kind of figure out how to pull some people together to find a solution for this it wasn't just specific to this particular use case I would love to work on that that would be awesome and and maybe this is a step Ben Schwartz I I wanna second that um as an example we just mentioned that maybe you need a new mandatory um service bindings parameter uh you know that probably is a totally general purpose parameter it's not DNR configuration it's like delegated credentials required um that could be applied anywhere even on the web and uh the but the the flip side of that is that the reason those cases have proven so hard is because it's very difficult to reason about the security properties of a certificate whose private key is two feet from the from the client um you know with whom you have you know ideally a good a good trust relationship ideally they are the the person the relying party but also they could be the attacker right uh so if you put one of these delegated credentials onto a box in my house then I can probably extract that delegate delegated credential and uh you know and now impersonate this thing and who knows you know what kind of interesting uh situations that that creates right so it's in a sense this it begins to look like an abuse of the ca system or an abuse of the pki and uh I would I'm not saying that's a show stopper but I'd like to see some some very thoughtful analysis and some some broader security expertise on okay on that question okay cool for people who saw me in the queue briefly it was because uh our A.D visibly blanched at the idea that DNR would be pulled back I I
